era_context: The Roman world continues to consolidate under Octavian's de facto rule. The Parthian Empire is in the midst of a succession crisis following Orodes II's death (c. 37 BCE). In China, the Western Han dynasty is in decline under Emperor Cheng, with Wang Mang rising in influence. In India, the Satavahana dynasty is expanding its power and maritime trade networks. This is a period of transition and consolidation across multiple civilizations, with the underlying structures of the ancient world being reshaped.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier accounts

events.title: Parthian succession crisis continues; Phraates IV and Mithridates III compete for throne
events.description: Following the death of Orodes II (c. 37 BCE), the Parthian Empire is in the midst of a succession crisis. Phraates IV and Mithridates III, both sons of Orodes II, are competing for control of the throne. By 38 BCE, the conflict is ongoing, with neither side able to achieve a decisive victory. This civil conflict weakens Parthian power and creates opportunities for Roman expansion in the Near East. The conflict will eventually be resolved in favor of Phraates IV by c. 32 BCE.
key_figures: Phraates IV (son of Orodes II, future King of Parthia)
Mithridates III (son of Orodes II, rival claimant)
events.certainty_note: The Parthian succession crisis is well-attested in numismatic and literary sources. The specific dating of events within the crisis to 38 BCE is approximate, based on numismatic evidence and the reign lengths of the contenders.

events.title: Octavian consolidates power; administrative reforms continue
events.description: Octavian continues his systematic consolidation of power and administrative reforms. By 38 BCE, he has secured control over the Mediterranean world and is preparing the groundwork for the constitutional settlement that will be formally announced in 27 BCE. The year 38 BCE is not marked by any single dramatic event, but rather by the continuation of the process that will transform the Republic into the Augustan principate.
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Octavian's consolidation is confirmed. Specific events of 38 BCE are not precisely dated in sources.
cross_references: Part of broader consolidation period (-35 to -27 BCE)

events.title: Emperor Cheng of Han continues weak rule; Wang Mang's influence grows
events.description: Emperor Cheng of the Western Han dynasty continues his reign, marked by weakness and dependence on court officials. Wang Mang, a relative of the imperial family, continues to accumulate power and influence. By 38 BCE, Wang Mang is effectively the dominant figure in the Han court, though the emperor retains nominal authority. This period of declining imperial power and rising official influence is characteristic of the late Western Han.
key_figures: Wang Mang (court official, c. 45 BCE–23 CE)

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian), continued by later historians

events.certainty_note: The decline of the Western Han and Wang Mang's rise are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The dating of specific events in 38 BCE is conventional.
cross_references: Part of broader Western Han decline

events.title: Satavahana maritime trade continues to expand; connections with Roman world deepen
events.description: The Satavahana dynasty continues to expand its control over maritime trade routes. By 38 BCE, Satavahana merchants are actively trading with Egypt and the Roman world, exchanging Indian spices, textiles, and gems for Roman wine, glassware, and other luxury goods. Archaeological evidence from ports like Barygaza shows increasing quantities of Roman pottery and coins, indicating the growth of this trade network.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Satavahana sites (pottery, coins, inscriptions)
Periplus of the Erythraean Sea (written c. 40–70 CE)

events.certainty_note: The expansion of Satavahana maritime trade is confirmed by archaeological evidence. The specific dating to 38 BCE is approximate, based on stratigraphic and numismatic evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Indian Ocean trade network expansion

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of 38 BCE as a year of stable consolidation under Octavian obscures the underlying violence and coercion. The Parthian succession crisis is a major event that disrupts the traditional narrative of stable empires. Modern scholarship emphasizes that the 'Augustan Peace' is a retrospective construct. Additionally, the dating of specific events to 38 BCE is often conventional; no contemporary sources pinpoint events to this exact year.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 38 BCE is similar to previous years: heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and China. The Parthian succession crisis is known primarily through Greek and Roman sources and numismatic evidence. Indian history relies on archaeological inference. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have no written records. The surviving sources are biased toward political and military history; economic and social history must be reconstructed from material evidence. Roman sources are triumphalist; Han sources reflect court perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Succession crisis follows from Orodes II's death
Succession crisis leads to Phraates IV's consolidation of power
Octavian's consolidation leads to constitutional settlement
Wang Mang's rise leads to usurpation in 9 BCE
